title: How Movers Handle Condominium Relocations in Addis
excerpt: Professional condo relocation flow: survey, scope, building coordination, crew briefing, lobby protection, loading, security IDs, unload, contingencies.
content:
How professional movers handle condominium relocations in Addis

A professional condominium move is a sequence: survey, scope document, building coordination, crew briefing, protected common-area transit, disciplined truck load, and room-placed unload. Here is how serious operators run it.

Pre-move survey

In person or video: inventory, fragile flags, disassembly needs, narrow turns, and distance from legal parking to the door. Estimators ask for management contacts when certificates or ID lists are required.

Written scope and crew sizing

Labor hours or flat fee, truck size, materials, stairs or long-carry assumptions, and cancellation terms appear in one estimate. Crew count matches volume and elevator round trips.

Building coordination

Office calls or emails confirm move date, elevator window, and paperwork. Some sites need insurance or indemnity forms filed days ahead.

Crew briefing on site

The lead walks the team: fragile pieces, elevator choice, padding plan, loading order, and neighbor-sensitive timing. Everyone hears the same plan.

Common-area protection

Floor runners, corner foam, and sometimes masonite in marble lobbies. Elevator pads when management requires them.

Loading order

Heavy stable bases low in the truck; fragile cartons top-weight without crush points. Mattresses and same-night essentials may load last for first-off delivery.

Security and IDs

Crew presents names matching the gate list. Substitutions are cleared with management before entry.

Unload and placement

Boxes placed by labeled room; furniture reassembled per contract; walkthrough with you before sign-off.

If the building stops the clock

Good companies communicate revised ETAs and document waiting time if contracts bill standby.

What you should still do

  • Keep valuables personal.
  • Monitor doors when propped open.
  • Photo walls before moving for deposit disputes.

Rain and lobby moisture

Mats and shoe covers reduce slip risk and marble water marks—crews should plan for wet seasons.

Night moves

If allowed, portable lighting on stairs; reflective gear at curbs—speed never overrides safety.

Conclusion

Condominium relocations succeed when movers treat the building like infrastructure, not an afterthought. The pattern is always coordinate early, protect shared space, load with discipline, and close with a documented walkthrough.
author: Active Movers Team
